_README: SYNTHETIC sample graph — entirely made up, no real third-party abstracts. Its only purpose is to let you smoke-test the pipeline (score_engagement.py -> build_lit_table.py) without first running a multi-hour crawl. The topic matches examples/issues_final.example.json ('ethics of self-tracking'). Copy this to ../citation_graph.json to try the scoring step. Replace with your real crawl output for actual use.

doi:10.0000/sample-001.title: The Quantified Self and the Limits of Self-Knowledge
doi:10.0000/sample-001.abstract: This invented abstract argues that continuous self-tracking promises richer self-knowledge but often substitutes metric salience for genuine self-understanding, reshaping which features of a life agents attend to. It is a fabricated example for testing the pipeline.

doi:10.0000/sample-002.title: Personal Data, Platforms, and the New Privacy Bargains
doi:10.0000/sample-002.abstract: A made-up abstract examining what obligations arise when self-tracked health data is shared with platforms, employers, and insurers, and whether consent does the normative work usually claimed for it. Entirely synthetic.

doi:10.0000/sample-003.title: Metrics, Meaning, and the Value of an Unmeasured Life
doi:10.0000/sample-003.abstract: This fabricated abstract asks whether reducing aspects of a life to numbers distorts what is valuable about them, drawing a contrast between instrumental and constitutive goods. Synthetic test data only.

doi:10.0000/sample-004.title: Autonomy, Nudges, and Self-Tracking Devices
doi:10.0000/sample-004.abstract: A synthetic abstract on whether self-tracking devices that nudge behavior enhance or erode autonomy, and how to tell the difference. Not a real paper.

doi:10.0000/sample-005.title: A Survey of Wearable Sensors (Engineering Background, Mostly Off-Topic)
doi:10.0000/sample-005.venue: Proceedings of the Synthetic Sensor Conference
doi:10.0000/sample-005.abstract: This invented abstract reviews wearable sensor hardware and battery life. It is included as an example of a paper that the crawl surfaces but that engages the ethical issues only marginally — useful for checking that low-relevance items score near zero.

doi:10.0000/sample-006.title: Surveillance Capitalism and the Self-Tracking Subject
doi:10.0000/sample-006.abstract: A fabricated abstract connecting self-tracking to broader debates about data extraction and power, with sustained discussion of privacy and a passing treatment of autonomy. Synthetic.

doi:10.0000/sample-007.title: Title-Only Example With No Abstract Anywhere
